source: All-In Podcast — E274: SpaceX $2T case, Nvidia shock selloff
summary: Recursive self-improvement and continual learning are the two remaining frontiers for AI capability, and unlocking them could make current 10x/year improvement estimates look conservative
summary_extended: Baker endorses Chamath's view that AI models are currently improving at roughly 10x per year in capability, and argues that two specific technical breakthroughs — recursive self-improvement (where a model has input into its own training during the forward pass) and continual learning (where a model learns from experience in real-time, analogous to human learning) — remain as the key unsolved frontiers. If either or both of these are unlocked, Baker suggests the current 10x/year improvement estimate could itself look conservative, implying an acceleration beyond what markets are currently pricing. He cites Andrej Karpathy joining Anthropic to lead pre-training efforts in this area as a highly significant signal that serious technical talent is being directed at these problems. The investment implication is a structural bullish view on AI capability trajectory, though Baker does not name specific equity beneficiaries in this segment, making it a thematic/macro view rather than a stock-specific thesis.
detail: Baker endorses Chamath's view that AI models are improving 10x per year and says recursive self-improvement (model has input into its own training during forward pass) combined with continual learning (model learns from experience like humans) could pull the future forward dramatically. Karpathy joining Anthropic to lead pre-training on this is seen as highly significant.
confidence_evidence: Baker uses hedged language ('might seem conservative if that comes to pass') but frames it as a structural view on AI trajectory
quote_fragment: 10Xing every year might seem conservative if that comes to pass
